name: Visual Vertigo Analogue Scale for Ataxia
description: A self-report visual analogue scale designed to assess the severity of vertigo, dizziness, and balance-related symptoms in patients with ataxia and vestibular disorders. Patients rate their symptoms on a continuous scale for various triggering situations.
instructions: For each situation listed below, please mark on the line how severe your symptoms of dizziness, unsteadiness, or vertigo are. The left end means no symptoms at all, and the right end means the worst symptoms you can imagine.

questions.question: Walking through a supermarket or shopping center
Being a passenger in a car
Watching action scenes on television or at the movies
Seeing busy visual patterns (stripes, checks, scrolling screens)
Walking on uneven or sloped surfaces
Walking in the dark or poorly lit areas
Standing or walking after quickly turning your head
General overall level of dizziness/unsteadiness today
How much do these symptoms affect your daily activities?

severityBands.label: Mild Symptoms
severityBands.description: Mild dizziness/vertigo with minimal impact on function.

interpretation.scoringMethod: Calculate the mean of all VAS ratings (0-10 scale). Individual items can also be analyzed separately to identify specific triggers.
interpretation.itemAnalysis: Items 1-7 assess specific triggering situations. Item 8 assesses overall symptom level. Item 9 assesses functional impact.
interpretation.visualVertigoDependence: High scores on items 3-4 (visual triggers) suggest visual vertigo/visual dependence which is common in cerebellar ataxia.
interpretation.clinicalUse: Used to track vestibular and balance symptoms in patients with ataxia, as well as to identify sensitivity to visual motion which may guide vestibular rehabilitation.
interpretation.minimumClinicallyImportantDifference: A change of 1.5-2 points is generally considered clinically meaningful.

references.citation: Dannenbaum E, Chilingaryan G, Fung J. Visual vertigo analogue scale: an assessment questionnaire for visual vertigo. J Vestib Res. 2011;21(3):153-159.
Guerraz M, Yardley L, Bertholon P, et al. Visual vertigo: symptom assessment, spatial orientation and postural control. Brain. 2001;124(Pt 8):1646-1656.

metadata.author: Adapted from Dannenbaum E et al.
